# SRD 4: Escalation and Review

One rule organizes it: every automated action carries a review budget set by its risk tier.

This section sets the envelope for the review loop. The connective tissue between the executor and the reviewer is the escalation ledger, which records each action's tier, its review budget, and its disposition. The ledger is the running matrix for the whole subsystem.

The demand side of the review gap is the executor: it produces actions faster than any reviewer can read them. The supply side is the reviewer pool, bounded by attention. The escalation ledger mediates the two. Each entry locates its rung on the risk ladder and states the claims posture for the action — what the executor asserts the action will and will not touch.

The sobering results from the pilot confirm the approach. Under heavy reviewer coupling, throughput collapsed; under light reviewer coupling, the escalation residue class grew without bound. The running matrix makes the trade visible: each row shows a claims posture and the review budget it consumed.

The governance attachments extend the base tiering. Each attachment binds a tier to its nearest standards hook, so an auditor can trace any disposition to the clause that authorized it. The structure-over-prompt argument applies here: the tiering lives in the ledger schema, not in the reviewer's judgment, which is remarkable given how much prior systems left to discretion.

The claims posture is the load-bearing element. An action with a stale claims posture re-enters the ledger at a higher rung. An action whose posture matches its diff exits the review loop early. The running matrix records both paths.
